coredata.dc:title: Encapsulation of thymol and menthol loaded N/S co-doped carbon dots derived from a mixture of herbal extracts as theranostic agents with anticancer properties
coredata.dc:creator: Sattariazar, Simin
Nejad Ebrahimi, Samad
Arsalani, Nasser
Kazeminava, Fahimeh
coredata.dc:description: This research was conducted by synthesizing carbon dots MNE-CDs (mixed natural extract-carbon dots) based on mixed natural extract (ginger, garlic, turmeric) through the hydrothermal routh. Menthol and thymol were loaded as multi-therapeutic drugs with the addition of the bio-enhancer loaded on MNE-CDs with the hydrothermal method during a separate stage. These nanostructures were successfully encapsulated in chitosan by the nanospray drying method to enhance sustainability and release control. This study answered three of these issues by fabricating novel carbon dots for anticancer potential, release behavior and bioimaging at the same time. Preparation carbon dots are characterized using UV–vis, PL, FE-SEM, DLS, EDX, and FT-IR analysis. A moderate and sustained release profile of encapsulated carbon dots was noticed in comparison to the free carbon dots over 48 h of study in both simulated physicological environment (pH 7.4) and tumor tissue (pH 5.2) conditions. It was found that the release of bioactive substances from encapsulated samples was significantly attenuated. The cell viability assay showed all the samples, including free and encapsulated carbon dots, offered acceptable cytotoxicity against MCF-7 breast cancer cells. Despite this, the toxicity of free carbon dots is more than the encapsulated samples, and also the enhancement in anticancer potential was not observed for carbon dots loaded with menthol and thymol. Upon the obtained results, the synthesized fluorescence N/S co-doped carbon dots hold great anticancer potential and biological fluorescent labeling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Therapeutic
Theranostic
Anti-cancer
Co-doped
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis MNE-CDs @ Menthol and MNE-CDs @ Thymol
Preparation encapsulation carbon dots (MNE-CDs/CS, MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS, MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS)
In vitro release behaviors of encapsulated and free carbon dots
In vitro MTT assay on MCF-7 human breast cancer cell lines for investigating the anticancer potential of free and encapsulated carbon dots
Anticancer assay and cellular uptake of free and encapsulated carbon dots
Ethics approval and consent to participate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The University of Tabriz financially supported this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Vis spectra of MNE-CDs, (b) UV-Vis spectra of MNE-CDs@ Menthol, (c) PL spectra of MNE-CDs, (d) PL spectra of MNE-CDs@ Menthol (e) MNE-CDs@ Thymol3.3. FT-IR analysis.
A) (a) FT-IR spectra of Menthol, (b) Thymol, (c) MNE-CDs, (d) MNE-CDs@ Thymol, (e) MNE-CDs@ Menthol, B) FT-IR spectra of (a) MNE-CDs (b) MNE-CDs@ Thymol (c) MNE-CDs@ Menthol.
FE-SEM image of MNE-CDs (a), MNE-CDs@ Menthol (b), and MNE-CDs@ Thymol (c) with corresponding EDX spectra.
Hydrodynamic size distribution of CDs-MNE (d), CDs-MNE@ Menthol (b), CDs-MNE@ Thymol (c).
FE-SEM image of encapsulated MNE-CDs/CS (a), MNE-CDs@Menthol/CS (b), MNE-CDs@ Thymol (c).

In vitro release of the active substance at both pH and both encapsulated and free state (a) MNE-CDs and MNE-CDs/CS (b) MNE-CDs@ Menthol and MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS, (c) MNE-CDs@Thymol and MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS.
Radical scavenging activity using the DPPH method at different concentration (A), Cell viability of free and encapsulated C-dots against MCF-7 cell line (B).
(A) Invert microscopic image of MCF-7 cells after incubation 1 h and 4 h with MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Thymol, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, (B) MNE-CDs/CS, MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS and MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS.
Illustration of the synthesis route of MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Thymol, MNE-CDs @ Menthol and encapsulated them.

head.ce:title: Encapsulation of thymol and menthol loaded N/S co-doped carbon dots derived from a mixture of herbal extracts as theranostic agents with anticancer properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Phytochemistry, Medicinal Plants and Drugs Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research was conducted by synthesizing carbon dots MNE-CDs (mixed natural extract-carbon dots) based on mixed natural extract (ginger, garlic, turmeric) through the hydrothermal routh. Menthol and thymol were loaded as multi-therapeutic drugs with the addition of the bio-enhancer loaded on MNE-CDs with the hydrothermal method during a separate stage. These nanostructures were successfully encapsulated in chitosan by the nanospray drying method to enhance sustainability and release control. This study answered three of these issues by fabricating novel carbon dots for anticancer potential, release behavior and bioimaging at the same time. Preparation carbon dots are characterized using UV–vis, PL, FE-SEM, DLS, EDX, and FT-IR analysis. A moderate and sustained release profile of encapsulated carbon dots was noticed in comparison to the free carbon dots over 48 h of study in both simulated physicological environment (pH 7.4) and tumor tissue (pH 5.2) conditions. It was found that the release of bioactive substances from encapsulated samples was significantly attenuated. The cell viability assay showed all the samples, including free and encapsulated carbon dots, offered acceptable cytotoxicity against MCF-7 breast cancer cells. Despite this, the toxicity of free carbon dots is more than the encapsulated samples, and also the enhancement in anticancer potential was not observed for carbon dots loaded with menthol and thymol. Upon the obtained results, the synthesized fluorescence N/S co-doped carbon dots hold great anticancer potential and biological fluorescent labeling.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a deadly disease that poses a severe risk to human health due to its high mortality and incidence [1–3]. Every year new strains of cancer are identified, so it is estimated that the number of cancer cases will increase to 22 million over the next two decades. Therefore, timely diagnosis and effective and safe treatment play an important role in cancer control [4].

Since cancer's inception, several treatments (chemotherapy, gene therapy, cell therapy, radiation therapy, and surgery) have been proposed. Studies have shown that although some of these methods have good results, they have several disadvantages [3,5]. Disadvantages of chemotherapy, including side effects and the unavailability of a sufficient amount of therapeutics for cancer cells, limit the efficiency of chemotherapy. On the other hand, cancer cells may show resistance to treatment, which can lead to a recurrence of the disease. Therefore, more research has been done on developing new therapies to battle cancer more effectively [6].

The emergence of fluorescent carbon dots, one of the most promising therapeutic agents, has created a new perspective on biomedicine and cancer [7–10]. Due to their optical properties and ability to be functionalized, the carbon dots can be a good option in treating cancer and other diseases. In addition to the properties mentioned above, carbon dots have unique properties such as biocompatibility, high fluorescent stability, and low toxicity. These factors make carbon dots susceptible to theranostic applications, such as bioimaging, sensing, and drug delivery [11–14].

Nano-theranostic is the science of applying nanosystems in the diagnosis and treatment of disease simultaneously. In recent years, theranostic has become a new science and appropriate treatment strategy for personalized medicine, molecular imaging and cancer research [15–17]. Compared to CT, MRI, and PET imaging techniques, bioimaging is preferred: the possibility of non-invasive imaging without skin damage, high stability, easy disposal, and low cost. Another feature of fluorescent imaging is the non-invasive tracking of drug delivery [18–20].

Carbon dots show unparalleled advantages in bioimaging due to their extraordinary physical and chemical properties. Since the discovery of carbon dots (2004) by Xu et al. [21], they have been exploited in various fields. Various parameters affect carbon dots performance and give direction to their applications: Surface passivation, carbon dots precursors, doped with hetero atom and carbon dots fabrication method [22,23]. According to previous reports, carbon dots have been prepared for medical applications in different ways with different precursors [24]. Natural resources have more advantages than expensive chemicals for producing single-doped and co-doped carbon dots. Doping is one of the most effective ways to regulate the intrinsic properties of carbon dots [25–27]. As mentioned, the existence of one or more types of heteroatoms divides carbon dots into two categories single and co-doped. There have been many reports that co-doped carbon dots are preferable to single-doped carbon dots, because they remove and ameliorate limitations of carbon dots doped with one type of heteroatom [28,29]. Although single-doped carbon dots have demonstrated superior potential for controlling the inherent properties of carbon dots, it also simultaneously includes limitations. The main limit is related to the light emission spectrum of carbon dots, which are confined to the yellow-blue range. The co-doped carbon dots overcome the existing limitations due to the synergistic effect between the doped hetero atoms and create a unique electronic structure. Three categories of the different doped atoms are nitrogen, nitrogen/sulfur(N/S), and nitrogen/phosphorus(N/P). In 2014 N/S co-doped carbon dots were synthesized via the hydrothermal method with α -lipoic Acid and ethylene diamine applied to detect Fe3+ [30], Kalytchuk et al. reported the application of N/S C-dots by hydrothermal treatment with highly luminescent L-Cysteine and citric acid in biomedical and biological systems [31].

Moreover, carbon dots are involved in the treatment of cancer in several ways as bioimaging, drug delivery system, and nanotherapeutic agent. Black pepper C-dots for tumor cell growth inhibition [32], p-dots as bioimaging and nanotherapeutic agent, C-CD/Tio2 for tumor detection and photothermal therapy of cancer cells [33], Gadolinium-doped carbon dots as nano-theranostic agents and many other cases [34]. In this study, we synthesized novel theranostic and self-targeting N/S co-doped carbon dots (CDs) based on the mixed natural extract of garlic, ginger, and turmeric. The mechanism of action of the synthesized carbon dots against the MCF-7 cell line is not fully understood, but it can be hypothesized based on the bioactive compounds present in the precursors used (polyphenols, phytoestrogens). One possible mechanism of action of the synthesized carbon dots against the MCF-7 cell line is related to phytoestrogens. Phytoestrogens are plant compounds that have a similar structure and function to estrogen, the female sex hormone. They can compete with endogenous estrogens (estrogens produced by the body) for binding to estrogen receptors. This can lead to the inhibition of cell growth and proliferation in hormone-dependent cancers, such as breast cancer. [35]. Therefore, they can be an excellent alternative drugs such as tamoxifen, used as estrogen receptor modulators with side effects. Also, studies have shown on the concomitant use of tamoxifen with natural compounds such as curcumin, which is the active ingredient of turmeric, inhibit cytochrome-mediated metabolism of tamoxifen to its active form which reduces its effectiveness [36,37]. In the present study, we delved into the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with a dual purpose simultaneous diagnosis and treatment while relying solely on natural compounds, thus bypassing the need for traditional anti-cancer drugs. The outcomes of this study provided compelling evidence of the substantial anticancer potential of these synthesized compounds, particularly against the MCF-7 cell line. Consequently, they present a viable alternative to conventional anti-cancer drugs, which are fraught with significant challenges in clinical use. These challenges stem from their propensity to induce dose-limiting side effects and their limited accumulation in tumor tissues.

EDA (Ethylenediamine) was purchased from Merck Co, PEG-1000 (polyethylene glycol), 2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl-hydrate (DPPH) free radical, thymol and menthol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. Chitosan Medium molecular weight from Aprin Co (Iran). Ginger, turmeric and garlic were prepared local market. Methanol, ethanol, hydrochloric acid, glacial acetic acid, and sulfuric acid 96% from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Luis, U.S.A.). UV–vis absorbance evaluation was performed using Shimadzu UV-2501 PC (Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence measurements were carried out using Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer LS45 (Waltham, USA). Transmission electron microscopies Philips EM 208 S (Eindhoven, Netherlands) images were recorded to assess C-dots size and morphology, and particle size was determined using dynamic light scattering (Zeta Sizer-ZS90, Malvern, UK). The surface morphology and elemental mapping were analyzed by field emission scattering electron microscopy and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FE-SEM, EDS MIRA3 TESCAN). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) equipped with ATR recorded spectra (Thermo Nicolet). The antioxidant test was evaluated by a microplate reader (EPOCH2 Bio Tec, USA). Nano-spray dryer (Tadbir Tarrah company, Mashhad, Iran).

The extract was prepared by the maceration method; in this step, fresh garlic, ginger, and turmeric were washed and cut into small pieces. After dried, samples were ground by using the blender to obtain the powder, a certain amount of garlic (10 g) soaked in distilled water, ginger (10 g) and turmeric (10 g) soaked in 70 % ethanol-water separately, in a ratio of 1–20 (w/v) of the sample to the solvent and placed on the stirrer overnight. The obtained extracts were then passed through a filter paper. Then, the solvent was evaporated by a rotary evaporator. Acquired extracts stored at 4 ℃.

MNE-CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal route, 1 g of mixed natural extract, 0.3 mg PEG, dispersed in 50 ml DI (Deionized) water, and placed on the stirrer for 20 min to obtain a uniform solution. Then the solution was transferred into a stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 4 h. To achieve maximum QY, EDA was added to the preparation of N/S Co-doped MNE-CDs. After cooling the autoclave at room temperature, a dark brown solution was obtained, which was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 30 min to separate the supernatant. CDs were purified after centrifuging with a dialysis bag (MW= 3.5 kDa) for 48 h in the dark against distilled water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis MNE-CDs @ Menthol and MNE-CDs @ Thymol
ce:section.ce:para:
Menthol and thymol loaded on CDs through a short-time hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, 0.2 g of thymol and menthol separately were added to 0.2 g MNE-CDs in 30 ml DI water, then transferred to a stainless autoclave at 180 ℃ for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solutions were dialyzed in DI water for 2 h using a membrane (MWCO: 3.5 KD) to remove the free menthol and thymol. The concentration of menthol and thymol in MNE-CDs were evaluated by measuring absorption at 220 and 274 nm, respectively, then matching them to standard curves. The loading amount was calculated based on equation 1:
loading
amount =
m
0
−
m
1
m
0
× 100
Where m
0 is the initial amount of drug, m
1 is the unloaded drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation encapsulation carbon dots (MNE-CDs/CS, MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS, MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS)
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, chitosan (CS) was dissolved at the concentration (1%) in 4% v/v glacial acetic acid, then the 50 vol of CS was added drop by drop with a syringe to 50 ml of each CDs with (1%) w/v, while on the stirrer at 1000 rpm. After, the specific amount of CaCl2 solution (0.2 %) w/v was added to the preparation mix and stirred for thirty minutes. Finally, prepared solutions are transferred to a Nano Spray Dryer apparatus and high-efficiency encapsulated products were obtained. ( Schematic 1).

To determine the Entrapment efficiency using the following Eq 2:
EE % =
(
Q
t
−
Q
r
)
Q
t
× 100
Where Qt is the total amount of the material to be encapsulated and Qr is the residue of the material amount recovered in aqueous solutions. Qr was determined by spectrophotometer after centrifuging the final solution.

The calculation of photoluminescent QY of carbon dots was performed by using quinine sulfate as standard, with the following Eq 3 [38]:
QY
CDs
=
QY
R
F
CDs
F
R
A
R
A
CDs
η
CDs
2
η
R
2
Where F is the emission area, A is the absorbance; all samples had adsorption less than 0.1. η is the refraction index of the solvent, R denotes the reference, which is the Quinine ̊sulfate with 54.6% QY. The excitation wavelength was 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release behaviors of encapsulated and free carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The release behavior of the prepared carbon dots as a therapeutic agent (MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, MNE-CDs@ Thymol) in both encapsulated and free cases were investigated in two pH (5.2, 7.4). Lyophilized carbon dots and encapsulated carbon dots (5 mg) were purred into a 2 ml buffer solution and transferred in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3.5 KD). afterward, the bag was immersed in 40 ml buffer at 37 ℃ and stirred at 50 rpm, 1 ml of desired released substance was removed at predetermined intervals and replaced with 1 ml buffer. The concentration of the released substances was calculated using a UV–vis device and following the prepared standard curves.

The radical scavenging potency of synthesized MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, and MNE-CDs@ thymol were evaluated according to the DPPH method [39,40], one of the most common methods for measuring antioxidant properties. DPPH (2, 2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) is a free and stable radical with a maximum absorbance of 517 nm. The mechanism of action of antioxidants is based on their ability to donate a hydrogen atom to a free radical, which causes the DPPH color to change from dark pink to yellow. This color change is measurable because it is based on the electron number absorbed in the reaction medium, different concentrations (4, 12, 20, 40, 80) µg.ml−1 of sample poured into a 96-well microplate and exposed to 70 µg.ml−1 methanolic solutions of DPPH. The percent antioxidant activity was calculated based on radical trapping by Eq 4:
radical
scavenging
activity % =
( Ac − As )
Ac
× 100

In this formula, Ac (absorbance of control) contained methanol and DPPH, except active substance, As (absorbance of the sample). Then Inhibition concentration (IC50) is estimated using a nonlinear regression algorithm, which refers to a sample concentration with a 50% inhibitory property. The absorbance evaluation at 517 nm was performed using a microplate reader Epoch 12 Bio Tek (Santa Clara, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro MTT assay on MCF-7 human breast cancer cell lines for investigating the anticancer potential of free and encapsulated carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro cell growth inhibition was assessed against the MCF-7 cell line; the MCF-7 cell line was cultured from the Pasteur Institute, Iran. Briefly, the MCF-7 breast cancer cells were seeded into a 96-well plate at a density of 2 × 104 cells per well in RPMI-1640 medium, including 10 % heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) incubated overnight at 37 ℃ in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. After that, the culture medium was removed, then washed with PBS. Then the cells were treated with various concentrations of MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, MNE-CDs@ Thymol and MNE-CDs/CS, MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS, MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS and incubated for 48 h. The culture medium was removed, then DMSO solvent (200 µl/well) was added to solubilize the formazan crystals. Then the MTT solution (5 mg/ ml) was added to the wells and stored for 4 h. The optical density (OD) was recorded by a microplate reader (Elx808, Bio Tek, USA) at 570 nm.

To evaluate the fluorescence imaging of MCF-7 cells treated with free carbon dots and encapsulated carbon dots, 2 ml (10 µg.ml−1) amount of each sample in phosphate-buffered saline PBS were added to the cell culture medium and incubated for 1 and 4 h at 37 ℃. The coverslips were placed onto the glass microscope slides. The cell photographs were taken using an inverted fluorescence microscopy (Olympus, Bh2-RFCA, Japan).

After reviewing and evaluating the quantum yields of S-doped carbon dots (11.72%) and N/S co-doped carbon dots (26.31 %), the N/S co-doped carbon dots (MNE-CDs) with higher quantum efficiency were selected for the next steps, including grafting with menthol and thymol, and encapsulation.

Fig. 1a, b relate to the UV–vis absorption spectrums with spectral rang 200–600 nm of (garlic, ginger, turmeric extracts, and MNE-CDs) and (MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, MNE-CDs@ Thymol) respectively. The UV spectra of garlic extract show a weak single peak at 279 nm that could be assigned to n→π * transition due to the presence of sulfur atoms. Two characteristic bands were observed in the turmeric extract spectrum at 257 nm and 426 nm is ascribed to π→π* and n→π⁕ transitions and the UV spectrum of ethanol extract of ginger shows a strong shoulder at 283 nm, which refers to transition π→π⁕ [41]. Fig. 1b shows the absorption spectra of prepared carbon dots; successful interaction of MNE-CDs with Menthol and Thymol was confirmed via comparing the spectra with each other, and the UV spectrum of MNE-CDs display two peaks at 285 nm and 349 nm, which belong to
π →
π
*
and
n →
π
*
transitions, respectively. In this case, the second peak at 349 nm is stronger than the first peak, While after conjugation with Thymol (MNE-CDs@ Thymol), the intensity of the second peak has decreased and the first peak has become sharper at the same wavelengths. In the MNE-CDs@ Menthol spectra, in addition to the absorptions at 285 nm and 349 nm, the MNE-CDs@ Menthol spectra show another absorption at 225 nm. Fig. 1(c-e) illustrates the PL (photoluminescence) spectra of MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, MNE-CDs@ Thymol, respectively, at the excitation range of 340–460 nm. The maximum emission belongs to MNE-CDs at 439 nm with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm; as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 to 460 nm, the emission intensity decreases and the photoluminescence emission wavelength shifts to higher wavelengths in all three cases. This phenomenon has been reported frequently in fluorescence nanocarbons. This could be due to different energy levels associated with various functional groups on the carbon dots' surface [42].

FT-IR analysis was performed to investigate functional groups and surface composition on prepared carbon dots. Fig. 2A shows the FT-IR spectrum of Menthol (a) Thymol (b) and synthesized carbon dots (MNE-CDs (c), MNE-CDs@ Thymol (d), MNE@ Menthol (e)). FT-IR spectrum of MNE-CDs (c) shows a broad peak at 3271 cm−1 attributed to O-H and N-H groups, C-H stretch appears at 2925 cm−1, and the peak at the 1660 cm−1 to 1595 cm−1 assigned to CN and CC groups, the appearance of a stretch vibrational band at 1130 cm−1 which due to the SO group give evidence for the presence of allicin, the active substance of garlic in CDs [43]. After grafted MNE-CDs with thymol (d), N-H and O-H bands have shifted from 3271 cm−1 to a higher frequency of 3362 cm−1 [44], and some of the bands disappeared after MNE-CDs were grafted with menthol (e), which corresponds to frequency 2925 cm−1. These changes in the spectra can be seen by comparing the bare spectra of menthol and thymol with the carbon dots spectra can be inter-related to prove the surface coating of menthol and thymol via electrostatic and covalent interaction. In Fig. 2B, three spectra related to encapsulation of MNE-CDs (a), MNE-CDs@ thymol (b), and MNE-CDs@ menthol (c) with chitosan, all of them have the characteristic band at 3350 cm−1 that related to stretching vibration of N-H which is the result of interaction between amine of chitosan and carboxyl of CDs.

To determine the changes in morphology and size resulting from the incorporation of MNE-CDs with Menthol and Thymol, we utilized field emission scanning electron microscopy and dynamic light scattering spectroscopy. In Fig. 3, SEM images are presented as follows: (a) MNE-CDs (18.84 ± 5.04), (b) MNE-CDs@ Menthol (27.05 ± 2.4), (c) MNE-CDs@ Thymol (23.04 ± 5.51). The average size of the carbon dots is provided, and corresponding particle size distribution diagrams are detailed in the Supporting information in Section S1. Notably, it is observed that the particle size of MNE-CDs is smaller before their incorporation with Menthol and Thymol, which is a reasonable outcome. Also, after surface grafting MNE-CDs with Menthol and Thymol, these nanoparticles still retained their spherical shape The elemental compositions was obtained using EDX analysis (Fig. 3a-c). the EDX result of CDs- MNE shows the presence of elements C, N, O, S with the amount of 29.01, 6.07, 35.01 and 29.91 respectively. The presence of sulfur in the synthesized carbon dots is proof of the successful synthesis of carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur. The increase in the percentage of carbon in CDs-MNE@Menthol and CDs-MNE@Thymol compounds compared to pristine CDs-MNE indicates the successful binding of thymol and menthol to the primary carbon dot.

The DLS technique characterized the hydrodynamic size distribution, and the acquired result is shown in Fig. 4. The size distribution of MNE-CDs (a) is (19.51 nm, PDI=0.45), MNE-CDs@ Menthol (b) is (82.1 nm, PDI=1), MNE-CDs@ Thymol (c) is (176.2, PDI=1.5), the particle size of carbon dots in DLS technique is slightly larger than SEM technique due to the cumulative property of carbon dots in the liquid medium. Zeta potential analysis was used to determine the electric charge of carbon dots particles and estimate their stability. ZP of MNE-CDs is − 18.9 Mv, ZP of MNE-CDs@ Menthol is − 11 mV, ZP of MNE-CDs@ Thymol is − 10 mV. The change in the amount of ZP can be attributed to the successful coating MNE-CDs with Menthol and Thymol.

Fig. 5 displays scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the encapsulated samples. The microstructures enclosed by chitosan walls are spherical, with some exhibiting surface depressions. The formation of such depressions in nanocapsules prepared using the spray-drying method is common and has been reported by other researchers [45]. The average diameter of the nanocapsules was determined as follows: (a) MNE-CDs/CS (621.93 nm), (b) MNE-CDs@Menthol/CS (659.92 nm), (c) MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS (722.64 nm). The corresponding particle size distribution diagrams are provided in the Supporting information in Section S2.

The in vitro release behavior of active ingredients from free and encapsulated carbon dots was investigated in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) with different pH values (pH= 7.4, 5.2) [46,47]. The loading amount percentage of menthol and thymol were calculated 64.40% and 68%, respectively. Furthermore, the carbon dots structure and loading method affect the loading degree. the release study was performed based on the active substance of each carbon dots. The release of encapsulated specimens is more controlled than that of non-encapsulated specimens. According to the calculations performed, the entrapment efficiency values for MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ thymol and MNE-CDs@ Menthol are as follows: 64 %, 58 %, 48 %. Fig. 6a shows both pH release profile of MNE-CDs and MNE-CDs/CS. Following the above, the release of the encapsulated samples at pH= 5.2 and 7.4 is slower than the free samples. It is obvious that release accelerates at an acidic pH, because of the existence of amino groups on the surface of chitosan that become protonated (a, A, pH= 5.2), as in the first 10 h have 20% release, under normal pH= 7.4 condition has 10% release. Fig. 6a C,(pH= 5.2) and D, (pH= 7.4) belongs to free MNE-CDs, which has a high release rate and the percentage that within 10 h 50 % of active substance have been released. Fig. 6b shows the release profile of MNE-CDs@ Menthol (A, pH= 5.2 and B, pH= 7.4) and MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS (C, pH= 5.2 and D, pH= 7.4).in this case, the stability time of encapsulated samples is longer than that of free samples. Fig. 6c represent release of MNE-CDs@ Thymol (A, pH=5.2 and B, pH= 7.4) and MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS (C, pH= 5.2 and D, pH=7.4).

The results of the radical scavenging activities in DPPH assays of carbon dots in different concentrations are shown in Fig. 7 (A). After Gallic acid as standard, MNE-CDs and MNE-CDs@ Menthol have the highest antioxidant activity, while MNE-CDs@ Thymol has the lowest activity in all concentrations. This assessment is completely consistent with the inhibitory concentration values. The inhibition concentration (IC50) of MNE-CDs is 48.73 µg.ml−1, MNE-CDs@ Menthol is 59 µg.ml−1, MNE-CDs@ Thymol is 144.68 µg.ml−1 and Gallic acid is 40.11 µg.ml−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anticancer assay and cellular uptake of free and encapsulated carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: We studied the cytotoxicity effect of bare and encapsulated carbon dots on breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7) proliferation by MTT assay. as shown in Fig. 7(B). Considerable toxicity was observed for both free and encapsulated carbon dots, In general, free carbon dots are more toxic than encapsulated samples with slight differences, especially CDs-MNE and CDs-MNE@Thymol. Cell viability of (MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@ Thymol, MNE-CDs@ Menthol, MNE-CDs/CS, MNE-CDs@ Thymol/CS, MNE-CDs@ Menthol/CS) was significantly reduced to 11.59 %, 11.69 %, 20.79 %, 20.36 %, 24.74 %, 48.52 % respectively, with the highest concentration 75 µg.ml−1. It's a widely recognized fact that encapsulated drugs often demonstrate enhanced effectiveness, particularly in scenarios involving the inhibition of cancer cell growth. However, it's crucial to acknowledge that the opposite outcome is entirely plausible. The encapsulation process can bring about alterations in the drug's properties, encompassing changes in solubility, stability, and distribution, all of which can influence its therapeutic efficacy. Probably, the incorporation of carbon dots into the chitosan matrices significantly reduces the scope of destruction by creating a rigid network. It was anticipated that the combination of thymol and menthol when delivered via carbon dots, could interact with each other and with cancer cells in a more potent manner compared to either compound on its own. Regarding the observed toxicity of carbon dots loaded with thymol and menthol, no substantial synergistic effect was detected.

The cell uptake and intracellular drug tracking were also confirmed in the inverted fluorescence microscopy images. As shown in Fig. 8 after incubating MCF-7 cells with free (A) and encapsulated carbon dots (B) by increasing the time from 1 to 4 h higher fluorescence was observed. here we can see that fluorescence of free carbon dots was clearly higher than encapsulated carbon dots. despite this, we can assume that the fluorescence of encapsulated carbon dots is no limitation to imaging applications with carbon dots. The results indicated the consequences of both events led to an increase in cell uptake.

In this work, N/S co-doped carbon dots (MNE-CDs, MNE-CDs@Menthol, MNE-CDs@Thymol) particles were designed and successfully synthesized with a view to efficient fluorescent, controlled release of active substances and anticancer potential against MCF-7 cell line. the cytotoxicity evaluation unveils that All carbon dots including pristine carbon dots, carbon dots loaded with menthol and thymol and encapsulated ones are toxic, which was predictable due to the chemical nature of the bioactive compounds present in the used precursor. While the encapsulated carbon dots were expected to have more effective anticancer properties, the results proved otherwise, free carbon dots were more toxic, and among them, pure carbon dots and thymol-loaded carbon dots were more toxic. According to the results, stable fluorescence properties and successful cellular internalization of free carbon dots make them suitable for tracking and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
The content of the manuscript has been approved by all the authors.

SS conceptualized and conducted the initial experiments, contributing to the creation of the manuscript's first draft. SNE and NA provided project supervision and participated in the manuscript's proofreading process. FK conducted the biological assays. All authors reviewed and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The University of Tabriz financially supported this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113603.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
